title: Use a consistent elevation system
summary: AI-generated interfaces apply shadows at varying intensities without a clear elevation system, making depth feel arbitrary rather than hierarchical.
instruction: Define a small set of elevation levels — Design Canon recommends 2-4 — mapped to semantic component roles (rest, interactive, overlay). Apply the same shadow values to all components at the same level. Most surfaces should have no elevation.
rationale: Shadows communicate what users can interact with and what is above the surface. When every element has a different shadow intensity, depth loses meaning. Material Design demonstrates elevation as a semantic system with defined levels; the specific number of levels should fit the project's complexity. Design Canon recommends keeping the elevation system small enough that each level has a distinct role.

violations: A card with 10px blur, floating button with 8px, modal with 24px, tooltip with 6px, dropdown with 12px -- five different elevations with no system.
An alert banner with heavier shadow than the modal it overlays, inverting expected depth hierarchy.
controls: Three elevations: level 0 (flat for all cards and panels), level 1 (4px blur on hover), level 2 (12px blur for modals and drawers).
A marketing page using zero shadows on all surfaces, depth communicated through color and size contrast.
borderline: A component library that needs 4-5 elevations for different interactive states (rest, hover, active, focus, drag) -- the key is that these map to state, not arbitrary values.

exceptions.description: Custom OS-level chrome (title bar overlays, system dialogs). 3D or spatial interfaces where depth is literal.

detector.signalDescription: Parse all box-shadow declarations, extract blur radius values, group by approximate level. Flag if more than 4 distinct elevation levels exist across the project.
limitations: Multiple comma-separated shadows on one element may create a single elevation effect from multiple blur values.
Tailwind-style utility shadows have defined blur values that map to semantic levels.
filter: drop-shadow() uses a different CSS mechanism not covered by box-shadow analysis.

scenarios: A button design using multiple shadows for a single elevation effect (ambient + directional).
A design system with 5 defined elevation tokens that only uses 2-3 in practice.
mitigations: Count distinct elevation levels (blur values within 15% tolerance as the same level), not raw blur values. Flag only when 5+ distinct groups exist.

fixturePlan.description: A page using shadows with blur values 4px, 8px, 10px, 16px, 20px, 24px across different components.
A page using shadow-sm, shadow, shadow-md, shadow-lg, shadow-xl where some are composition shadows.
